arguments.prompt: segment objects based on intensity

response: ij3d-segmentation-spot3d-local-mean

The node works with two images, one containing the seeds of the objects, that can be obtained from local maxima (see 3D Filters or 3D Maxima Finder), the other image containing signal data. The program computes a local threshold around each seeds and cluster voxels with values higher than the local threshold computed. Three circles are drawn. The user defines the radius (in pixels) of each
circle, given that the first circle should be located within the object, while
the two other circles should be located outside of the object. The mean
intensities of the object (within the first circle), and of the background (in
between the two other circles) are measured and the threshold calculated.
By default, the threshold is the mean of the two mean intensity values
(weight=0.5). The user can however shift this parameter: for a weight
value of 0.75, the threshold will be closer to the background value.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

response: trackmate-create-spot-tracker-overlap-tracker

This tracker is a simple extension of the Intersection - over - Union (IoU) tracker.

It generates links between spots whose shapes overlap between consecutive frames. When several spots are eligible as a source for a target, the one with the largest IoU is chosen.

The minimal IoU parameter sets a threshold below which links won't be created. The scale factor allows for enlarging (>1) or shrinking (<1) the spot shapes before computing their IoU. Two methods can be used to compute IoU: The Fast one approximates the spot shapes by their rectangular bounding-box. The Precise one uses the actual spot polygon.

This tracker works in 2D and 3D. However in 3D, the IoU is computed from the bounding-boxes regardless of the choice of the IoU computation method. The Precise method is not implemented.

response: ij1-feature-featurej-structure

Computes for each image element (pixel/voxel) the eigenvalues of the so-called structure tensor, which can be used for example in texture analysis and image enhancement filtering. Due to the positive-semi-definiteness of the structure tensor, the eigenvalues are always larger than or equal to zero. If the size of the image is unity in the z-dimension (single slice), the node computes 2D-tensor eigenvalues, otherwise it computes 3D-tensor eigenvalues (for each time frame and channel in a 5D image).

arguments.prompt: detect objects in an image

response: ij-multi-template-matching

Template matching is an algorithm that can be used for object-detections in grayscale images. To perform template matching you will need a template image that is searched in the target image.
The best is to simply crop a typical region of interest from a representative image.



The algorithm computes the probability to find one (or several) template images provided by the user into a larger image. The algorithm uses the template image as a sliding window translated over the image, and at each position of the template computes a similarity score between the template and the image patch.
This results in a correlation map, for which the pixel value at position (x,y) is proportional to the similarity between the template and image patch, at this (x,y) position in the image. The computation of the correlation map is followed by extrema detection to list the possible locations of the template in the image. Each extrema corresponds to the possible location of a bounding box of dimensions identical to the template. The extrema detection can list a large number of extrema in the first place. Usually a threshold on the score is then used to limit this number (i.e. returning local extrema with a score above/below the threshold).
To prevent overlapping detection of the same object, Non-Maxima Supression is performed after extrema detection.



Please visit https://github.com/multi-template-matching/MultiTemplateMatching-Fiji/wiki for more information about the Multi-Template Matching plugin.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of multiple data items. If you want to group all data into one output, set the matching strategy to 'Custom' and leave 'Data set matching annotations' empty.

arguments.prompt: track objects in a time-series of images

response: trackmate-create-spot-tracker-advanced-kalman-tracker

This tracker is an extended version of the Kalman tracker, that adds the possibility to customize linking costs and detect track fusion (segments merging) and track division (segments splitting).

This tracker is especially well suited to objects that move following a nearly constant velocity vector. The velocity vectors of each object can be completely different from one another. But for the velocity vector of one object need not to change too much from one frame to another.

In the frame-to-frame linking step, the classic Kalman tracker infer most likely spot positions in the target frame from growing tracks and link all extrapolated positions against all spots in the target frame, based on the square distance. This advanced version of the tracker allows for penalizing links to spots with different features values using the same framework that of the LAP tracker in TrackMate. Also, after the frame-to-frame linking step, track segments are post-processed to detect splitting and merging events, and perform gap-closing. This is again based on the LAP tracker implementation.

arguments.prompt: track objects in a time-series of images

response: trackmate-create-spot-tracker-advanced-kalman-tracker

This tracker is an extended version of the Kalman tracker, that adds the possibility to customize linking costs and detect track fusion (segments merging) and track division (segments splitting).

This tracker is especially well suited to objects that move following a nearly constant velocity vector. The velocity vectors of each object can be completely different from one another. But for the velocity vector of one object need not to change too much from one frame to another.

In the frame-to-frame linking step, the classic Kalman tracker infer most likely spot positions in the target frame from growing tracks and link all extrapolated positions against all spots in the target frame, based on the square distance. This advanced version of the tracker allows for penalizing links to spots with different features values using the same framework that of the LAP tracker in TrackMate. Also, after the frame-to-frame linking step, track segments are post-processed to detect splitting and merging events, and perform gap-closing. This is again based on the LAP tracker implementation.

Runs Cellpose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value. Please note that ImageJ will run into issues if too many objects are present and the labels are saved in int32. ImageJ would load them as float32.
  • Flows: An RGB image that indicates the flow of each pixel. Convert it to HSB to extract information.
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the probabilities for each pixel.
  • Styles: A vector summarizing each image.
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

  • Flows: An RGB image that indicates the flow of each pixel. Convert it to HSB to extract information.
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the probabilities for each pixel.
  • Styles: A vector summarizing each image.
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

Produces a touch-matrix where the n nearest neighbors are marked as touching neighbors.

Takes a distance matrix (e.g. derived from a pointlist of centroids) and marks for every column the n smallest
distances as neighbors. The resulting matrix can be use as if it was a touch-matrix (a.k.a. adjacency graph matrix).

Inspired by a similar implementation in imglib2 [1]

Note: The implementation is limited to square matrices.

Parameters
----------
distance_marix : Image
touch_matrix_destination : Image
n : int
number of neighbors

References
----------
[1] https://github.com/imglib/imglib2/blob/master/src/main/java/net/imglib2/interpolation/neighborsearch/InverseDistanceWeightingInterpolator.java


Parameters: Image distance_matrix, ByRef Image touch_matrix_destination, Number n

ccdbe8fe-1a1c-4364-a656-39de2195aed8.prompt: I want to analyze a time-lapse microscopy video and quantify the changes in fluorescence intensity within specific regions of interest over time, but I need to automatically detect those regions of interest in each frame. Can you create a JIPipe pipeline that automatically identifies ROIs, measures fluorescence within those ROIs in each frame, and outputs the intensity values as a time-series for each ROI?

<tool_call>
{"name": "get_node_name", "arguments": {"prompt": "ROI validation based on fluorescence intensity changes over time"}}
</tool_call><|im_end|>
model_message.thinking:
To further develop the pipeline for analyzing a time-lapse microscopy video and quantifying fluorescence intensity changes within specific regions of interest (ROIs), we need to consider how to automatically detect these ROIs in each frame. Given that the previous steps have focused on identifying ROIs and measuring fluorescence within them, the next logical step involves ensuring that the detection of ROIs is robust and can be applied consistently across all frames of the time-lapse video.

Reflecting on the current state of the pipeline, it seems we are on the verge of needing a mechanism that can not only detect ROIs but also validate their presence and relevance within the context of the time-lapse data. This suggests that our next action should involve incorporating a node that can assess the validity of the ROIs detected, potentially by comparing them against a predefined set of criteria or by utilizing a method that can identify meaningful changes in the fluorescence intensity over time.

To achieve this, we should aim to find a node that can perform such validation or assessment. The description of the desired functionality for this node should include terms like "ROI validation," "fluorescence intensity change detection," or "time-lapse analysis." By using the 'get_node_name' tool with an appropriate prompt, we can identify a node that matches this description and is capable of providing the necessary validation for the ROIs detected in each frame.

Therefore, the next step involves preparing a prompt for the 'get_node_name' tool that describes the need for a node capable of validating ROIs based on fluorescence intensity changes over time, which will guide us in selecting the most suitable node for inclusion in our pipeline. This approach ensures that our pipeline not only detects ROIs but also critically evaluates them, leading to more accurate and meaningful analysis of the time-lapse microscopy data.

arguments.prompt: ROI validation based on fluorescence intensity changes over time

response: ij1-roi-extract-profile

Extracts the pixel intensities along the ROI if a straight or irregular line ROI are given. If a rotated rectangle is processed, it is converted into a straight line.If any other ROI type is given, either the row or column average of the bounding rectangle is calculated.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

arguments.prompt: segment cells in microscopy images

response: ij3d-segmentation-nuclei-3d

This plugin is designed to segment nuclei from cell culture (not from tissues). The method is based on a maximum Z-projection followed by a 2D Segmentation. The segmentation for the 2D projection is based on a global thresholding. The nuclei are then segmented and separated using ImageJ watershed.

2e210d26-88ad-4855-9ee7-9935d87d918a.prompt: I want to measure the area and circularity of multiple objects in an image, but I need to filter out objects based on their size before the measurements are taken. Can you create a JIPipe pipeline that segments objects, filters them based on area, and then measures the area and circularity of the remaining objects?

model_message.content:
<tool_call>
{"name": "get_node_name", "arguments": {"prompt": "Segment objects based on their size"}}
</tool_call><|im_end|>
model_message.thinking:
To proceed with building the JIPipe pipeline as per the user's requirements, we need to consider how to effectively segment objects in the image. Given that the user wants to filter out objects based on their size before measurements are taken, it's crucial to have a method that can identify and isolate these objects from the background.

Reflecting on the previous steps and the current state of the pipeline, we recognize that we have already included a node for segmenting objects. However, to ensure that the segmentation process is efficient and aligns with the user's needs for filtering by size, we should verify if the current node indeed provides the necessary functionality.

Before adding any new nodes or modifying existing ones, it would be prudent to confirm that the segmentation node can handle the task of identifying objects based on their size, which is essential for subsequent filtering and measurement processes.

Therefore, the next logical step involves utilizing a tool to retrieve the name and description of a node that matches the functionality of segmenting objects based on their size. This will help in validating whether the current pipeline includes an appropriate node for this specific task or if an alternative node needs to be considered.

The intention here is to ensure that the pipeline's foundation is solid by confirming the suitability of the segmentation node before proceeding with further steps like filtering and measurement.

arguments.prompt: Segment objects based on their size

response: ij3d-segmentation-spot3d-local-mean

The node works with two images, one containing the seeds of the objects, that can be obtained from local maxima (see 3D Filters or 3D Maxima Finder), the other image containing signal data. The program computes a local threshold around each seeds and cluster voxels with values higher than the local threshold computed. Three circles are drawn. The user defines the radius (in pixels) of each
circle, given that the first circle should be located within the object, while
the two other circles should be located outside of the object. The mean
intensities of the object (within the first circle), and of the background (in
between the two other circles) are measured and the threshold calculated.
By default, the threshold is the mean of the two mean intensity values
(weight=0.5). The user can however shift this parameter: for a weight
value of 0.75, the threshold will be closer to the background value.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

Runs Cellpose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value. Please note that ImageJ will run into issues if too many objects are present and the labels are saved in int32. ImageJ would load them as float32.
  • Flows: An RGB image that indicates the flow of each pixel. Convert it to HSB to extract information.
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the probabilities for each pixel.
  • Styles: A vector summarizing each image.
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: segment cells by intensity differences

response: cellpose

Runs Cellpose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value. Please note that ImageJ will run into issues if too many objects are present and the labels are saved in int32. ImageJ would load them as float32.
  • Flows: An RGB image that indicates the flow of each pixel. Convert it to HSB to extract information.
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the probabilities for each pixel.
  • Styles: A vector summarizing each image.
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

184befd0-f7aa-47b3-82cb-230fcc303d7f.prompt: I want to quantify the spatial distribution of fluorescent particles in my images. Can you create a JIPipe pipeline that detects these particles, calculates their spatial coordinates, and then generates a heat map visualizing their density and distribution across the image?

model_message.content:
<tool_call>
{"name": "get_node_name", "arguments": {"prompt": "detect fluorescent particles"}}
</tool_call><|im_end|>
model_message.thinking:
To further develop the pipeline for quantifying the spatial distribution of fluorescent particles in images, we need to consider how to effectively detect these particles. Given the previous steps that have focused on generating a heat map, it's crucial to first identify the particles themselves. 

Reflecting on the current state of the pipeline, we recognize that we have not yet included a node for detecting fluorescent particles. This step is essential because without detecting the particles, we cannot proceed with calculating their spatial coordinates or generating a heat map.

To achieve this, we should aim to find a node that can detect fluorescent particles based on their fluorescence intensity. The description of such a node would ideally match our requirement for detecting particles in the image. 

Therefore, our next step should involve using a tool to find a node that fits this description. We will utilize the 'get_node_name' tool by providing a prompt such as "detect fluorescent particles" to retrieve the name and description of the most suitable node for this task. 

Upon receiving the response from the 'get_node_name' tool, we can assess whether the suggested node aligns with our needs for detecting fluorescent particles. If it does, we can proceed to include this node in our pipeline, ensuring that it connects appropriately with the existing nodes to facilitate the subsequent steps of calculating spatial coordinates and generating a heat map.

This approach ensures that we are methodically building our pipeline by first addressing the critical need of particle detection, which is a prerequisite for all subsequent analyses.

arguments.prompt: detect fluorescent particles

response: trackmate-create-spot-tracker-sparse-lap-tracker

This tracker is based on the Linear Assignment Problem mathematical framework.
Its implementation is adapted from the following paper:
Robust single-particle tracking in live-cell time-lapse sequences -
Jaqaman et al., 2008, Nature Methods.

Tracking happens in 2 steps: First spots are linked from frame to frame to
build track segments. These track segments are investigated in a second step
for gap-closing (missing detection), splitting and merging events.

Linking costs are proportional to the square distance between source and
target spots, which makes this tracker suitable for Brownian motion.
Penalties can be set to favor linking between spots that have similar
features.

Solving the LAP relies on the Jonker-Volgenant solver, and a sparse cost matrix formulation, allowing it to handle very large problems.

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.

  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.

arguments.prompt: Segment individual cells in microscopy images

response: ij3d-segmentation-nuclei-3d

This plugin is designed to segment nuclei from cell culture (not from tissues). The method is based on a maximum Z-projection followed by a 2D Segmentation. The segmentation for the 2D projection is based on a global thresholding. The nuclei are then segmented and separated using ImageJ watershed.

response: omnipose

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

response: ij3d-segmentation-nuclei-3d

This plugin is designed to segment nuclei from cell culture (not from tissues). The method is based on a maximum Z-projection followed by a 2D Segmentation. The segmentation for the 2D projection is based on a global thresholding. The nuclei are then segmented and separated using ImageJ watershed.

arguments.prompt: object segmentation

response: ij3d-segmentation-spot3d-local-mean

The node works with two images, one containing the seeds of the objects, that can be obtained from local maxima (see 3D Filters or 3D Maxima Finder), the other image containing signal data. The program computes a local threshold around each seeds and cluster voxels with values higher than the local threshold computed. Three circles are drawn. The user defines the radius (in pixels) of each
circle, given that the first circle should be located within the object, while
the two other circles should be located outside of the object. The mean
intensities of the object (within the first circle), and of the background (in
between the two other circles) are measured and the threshold calculated.
By default, the threshold is the mean of the two mean intensity values
(weight=0.5). The user can however shift this parameter: for a weight
value of 0.75, the threshold will be closer to the background value.

arguments.prompt: Identify and track dividing cells based on their division markers across frames.

response: omnipose

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: Find a node that can track cells across frames in time-lapse microscopy data, providing outputs for speed, direction, and shape descriptors.

response: omnipose

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: Find a node that can calculate speed, direction, and shape descriptors from cell tracking data.

response: omnipose

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.

arguments.prompt: Find a node that can calculate speed, direction, and shape descriptors from cell tracking data.

response: omnipose

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

arguments.prompt: track cells based on morphological features

response: omnipose

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

response: ij1-analyze-find-particles2d

Converts mask images into ROI by applying a connected components algorithm and generates measurements. Please note that this algorithm will always trace the external object boundaries and convert them into polygonal ROIs. This means that holes will be closed. This node requires a thresholded image as input and will extract measurements from the thresholded image. If you already have ROI available and want measurements, use 'Extract ROI statistics'.If higher-dimensional data is provided, the results are generated for each 2D slice.

response: ij3d-segmentation-nuclei-3d

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: Accurately segment fluorescent protein and organelle in each frame

response: omnipose

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

Applies a non-local means filter using a box neighborhood with a Gaussian weight specified with sigma to the input image.

Available for: 2D, 3D

arguments.prompt: segment fluorescent protein and organelle

response: ij3d-segmentation-nuclei-3d

This plugin is designed to segment nuclei from cell culture (not from tissues). The method is based on a maximum Z-projection followed by a 2D Segmentation. The segmentation for the 2D projection is based on a global thresholding. The nuclei are then segmented and separated using ImageJ watershed.

This plugin is designed to segment nuclei from cell culture (not from tissues). The method is based on a maximum Z-projection followed by a 2D Segmentation. The segmentation for the 2D projection is based on a global thresholding. The nuclei are then segmented and separated using ImageJ watershed.

2817e370-19ed-4e6d-8245-d71e1ce3a0f5.prompt: I have a time-series of images showing the expression of a fluorescent protein in cells, and I want to quantify the co-localization between this protein and a specific organelle within those cells over time. However, the signal from both the protein and the organelle is often weak and noisy. Can you create a JIPipe pipeline that first applies denoising filters to enhance the signal, then accurately segments both the fluorescent protein and the organelle in each frame, and finally calculates a co-localization coefficient (like Pearson's or Manders') for each cell at each time point, outputting a time-series of co-localization values for each cell?

arguments.prompt: apply denoising filter

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient for each cell at each time point

response: ij1-colocalization-coloc2

Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

model_message.thinking:
To address the user's request for a JIPipe pipeline that quantifies co-localization between a fluorescent protein and a specific organelle over time, we need to consider the main steps involved in achieving this goal. The user has specified that the signal from both the protein and the organelle is often weak and noisy, which suggests that the pipeline should include a denoising step to enhance the signal before proceeding with segmentation and co-localization calculations.

Given the current state of the pipeline, which includes a node for applying denoising filters to enhance the signal, our next step should focus on accurately segmenting both the fluorescent protein and the organelle in each frame. This segmentation is crucial because it allows for the precise identification and analysis of the co-localization between these two entities.

To achieve accurate segmentation, we should consider using a node that can perform this task effectively. The description of the node we are aiming for should include the ability to segment both the fluorescent protein and the organelle, which aligns with the user's requirements. 

Before adding this node to our pipeline, it would be beneficial to verify that the node we are considering can indeed perform the required segmentation. This verification can be done by using a tool that matches the node's description with the desired functionality, such as the 'get_node_name' tool. By providing a prompt that describes the desired functionality (e.g., "segment both fluorescent protein and organelle"), we can obtain the name and description of the node that best fits this requirement.

Once we have identified the appropriate node for segmentation, we should also consider how it connects with the existing nodes in our pipeline. This involves checking if the output of the denoising filter node can be directly used as input to the segmentation node, ensuring a smooth flow of data through the pipeline.

Therefore, our next action should be to prepare a prompt for the 'get_node_name' tool that describes the desired functionality of a node capable of segmenting both the fluorescent protein and the organelle. This will help us identify the most suitable node for our pipeline and proceed with adding it to our list of nodes.

arguments.prompt: segment both fluorescent protein and organelle

response: ij3d-segmentation-nuclei-3d

This plugin is designed to segment nuclei from cell culture (not from tissues). The method is based on a maximum Z-projection followed by a 2D Segmentation. The segmentation for the 2D projection is based on a global thresholding. The nuclei are then segmented and separated using ImageJ watershed.

model_message.thinking:
To further develop the JIPipe pipeline for quantifying co-localization between a fluorescent protein and a specific organelle over time, we need to consider how to accurately calculate the co-localization coefficient for each cell at each time point. The user has specified that the signal from both the protein and the organelle is often weak and noisy, which suggests that the pipeline should include a denoising step to enhance the signal before proceeding with segmentation and co-localization calculations.

Given the current state of the pipeline, which includes a node for applying denoising filters to enhance the signal and another node for segmenting both the fluorescent protein and the organelle, our next step should focus on accurately calculating the co-localization coefficient for each cell at each time point.

To achieve this, we should consider using a node that can perform this task effectively. The description of the node we are aiming for should include the ability to calculate a co-localization coefficient (like Pearson’s or Manders’) for each cell at each time point.

Before adding this node to our pipeline, it would be beneficial to verify that the node we are considering can indeed perform the required co-localization calculation. This verification can be done by using a tool that matches the node's description with the desired functionality, such as the 'get_node_name' tool. By providing a prompt that describes the desired functionality (e.g., "calculate co-localization coefficient for each cell at each time point"), we can obtain the name and description of the node that best fits this requirement.

Once we have identified the appropriate node for co-localization calculation, we should also consider how it connects with the existing nodes in our pipeline. This involves checking if the output of the segmentation node can be directly used as input to the co-localization calculation node, ensuring a smooth flow of data through the pipeline.

Therefore, our next action should be to prepare a prompt for the 'get_node_name' tool that describes the desired functionality of a node capable of calculating a co-localization coefficient for each cell at each time point. This will help us identify the most suitable node for our pipeline and proceed with adding it to our list of nodes.

In summary, the main steps we are taking to develop the JIPipe pipeline include:
1. Applying denoising filters to enhance the signal.
2. Accurately segmenting both the fluorescent protein and the organelle in each frame.
3. Calculating a co-localization coefficient (like Pearson’s or Manders’) for each cell at each time point.

By following these steps, we aim to create a pipeline that effectively quantifies the co-localization between the fluorescent protein and the specific organelle over time, addressing the user's requirements for handling weak and noisy signals.

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient for each cell at each time point

response: ij1-colocalization-coloc2

Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

0a11eb14-5ebf-4c29-88e4-41f821fc4f4f.prompt: I have a time-series of images showing the expression of a fluorescent protein in cells, and I want to quantify the co-localization between this protein and a specific organelle within those cells over time. However, the signal from both the protein and the organelle is often weak and noisy. Can you create a JIPipe pipeline that first applies denoising filters to enhance the signal, then accurately segments both the fluorescent protein and the organelle in each frame, and finally calculates a co-localization coefficient (like Pearson’s or Manders’) for each cell at each time point, outputting a time-series of co-localization values for each cell?

arguments.prompt: apply specific algorithm for segmentation of fluorescent protein and organelle

response: clij:plugin:net.haesleinhuepf.clijx.clij2wrappers.VoronoiOtsuLabeling

Labeles objects directly from grey-value images.

The two sigma parameters allow tuning the segmentation result. The first sigma controls how close detected cells can be (spot_sigma) and the second controls how precise segmented objects are outlined (outline_sigma).Under the hood, this filter applies two Gaussian blurs, spot detection, Otsu-thresholding and Voronoi-labeling.
The thresholded binary image is flooded using the Voronoi approach starting from the found local maxima.
Noise-removal sigma for spot detection and thresholding can be configured separately.

Parameters: Image input, ByRef Image destination, Number spot_sigma, Number outline_sigma
Available for: 2D, 3D



Please be aware that this node was generated automatically by JIPipe from existing interfaces provided by CLIJ. Please contact the JIPipe developers if you find any issues.

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient for each cell at each time point

response: ij1-colocalization-coloc2

Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

arguments.prompt: apply specific algorithm for segmentation of fluorescent protein and organelle

response: clij:plugin:net.haesleinhuepf.clijx.clij2wrappers.VoronoiOtsuLabeling

Labeles objects directly from grey-value images.

The two sigma parameters allow tuning the segmentation result. The first sigma controls how close detected cells can be (spot_sigma) and the second controls how precise segmented objects are outlined (outline_sigma).Under the hood, this filter applies two Gaussian blurs, spot detection, Otsu-thresholding and Voronoi-labeling.
The thresholded binary image is flooded using the Voronoi approach starting from the found local maxima.
Noise-removal sigma for spot detection and thresholding can be configured separately.

Parameters: Image input, ByRef Image destination, Number spot_sigma, Number outline_sigma
Available for: 2D, 3D



Please be aware that this node was generated automatically by JIPipe from existing interfaces provided by CLIJ. Please contact the JIPipe developers if you find any issues.

arguments.prompt: perform accurate segmentation of protein and organelle in each frame

response: omnipose

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient for each cell at each time point

response: ij1-colocalization-coloc2

Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

arguments.prompt: apply denoising filters to improve image quality

response: ij1-noise-despeckle2d

arguments.prompt: perform accurate segmentation of protein and organelle in each frame

response: omnipose

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient for each cell at each time point

response: ij1-colocalization-coloc2

Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

The signal is weak and noisy. Can you design a JIPipe pipeline that first applies denoising filters, then accurately segments both the protein and the organelle in each frame, and finally calculates a co-localization coefficient (like Pearson's or Manders') for each cell at each time point, outputting a time-series of co-localization values for each cell?

arguments.prompt: segment protein and organelle in each frame

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient between two images

response: ij1-colocalization-coloc2

arguments.prompt: perform accurate segmentation of protein and organelle in each frame

response: omnipose

Deprecated. Use the node with the same name.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

15b7841c-f39f-40e0-8102-c0484c6dccfb.prompt: I have a time-series of images showing the expression of a fluorescent protein in cells, and I want to quantify the co-localization between this protein and a specific organelle over time. The signal is weak and noisy. Can you design a JIPipe pipeline that first applies denoising filters, then accurately segments both the protein and the organelle in each frame, and finally calculates a co-localization coefficient (like Pearson’s or Manders’) for each cell at each time point, outputting a time-series of co-localization values?

To address the user's request for a JIPipe pipeline that quantifies co-localization between a fluorescent protein and a specific organelle over time, we need to consider the main steps involved in achieving this goal. The user has specified that the signal is weak and noisy, which implies the need for initial denoising before proceeding with segmentation and co-localization calculations.

Given the current state of the pipeline, which includes a node for applying denoising filters to the input images, our next step should focus on accurately segmenting both the protein and the organelle in each frame. This segmentation is crucial because it directly impacts the accuracy of the co-localization coefficient calculation.

To achieve accurate segmentation, we should look for a node that can perform this task effectively. The description of the desired functionality should include terms like "segmentation," "cell," "organelle," or similar, indicating that the node can handle the segmentation of biological structures within images.

The prompt for this tool call could be something like "node that can accurately segment both protein and organelle in each frame."

If it does, we can proceed to add this node to our pipeline, ensuring that the flow of data and the overall functionality of the pipeline align with the user's requirements.

This step is crucial because it directly addresses the user's need for accurate segmentation before calculating co-localization, which is essential for obtaining meaningful results from the time-series of images.

arguments.prompt: node that can accurately segment both protein and organelle in each frame

response: ij3d-segmentation-nuclei-3d

This plugin is designed to segment nuclei from cell culture (not from tissues). The method is based on a maximum Z-projection followed by a 2D Segmentation. The segmentation for the 2D projection is based on a global thresholding. The nuclei are then segmented and separated using ImageJ watershed.

arguments.prompt: segment both protein and organelle in each frame

response: cellpose-inference-2.x

Runs Cellpose on the input image with the given model(s). This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient between protein and organelle

response: ij1-colocalization-coloc2

Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

response: cellpose-inference-2.x

Runs Cellpose on the input image with the given model(s). This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

model_message.thinking:
To further develop the JIPipe pipeline that quantifies co-localization between a fluorescent protein and a specific organelle over time, we need to consider the main steps involved in achieving this goal. The user has specified that the signal is weak and noisy, which implies the need for initial denoising before proceeding with segmentation and co-localization calculations.

Given the current state of the pipeline, which includes a node for applying denoising filters to the input images and a node for accurately segmenting both the protein and the organelle in each frame, our next step should focus on calculating the co-localization coefficient for each cell at each time point.

The description of the desired functionality should include terms like "co-localization," "Pearson’s," "Manders’," or similar, indicating that the node can perform these specific calculations. This is crucial because it directly impacts the accuracy and relevance of the results obtained from the time-series of images.

Before adding this node to the pipeline, it would be beneficial to verify if the output of the current segmentation node is compatible with the input requirements of the co-localization calculation node. This compatibility check ensures that the pipeline can flow logically from one step to the next without any issues.

Therefore, our next action should involve using a tool to find a node that matches the description of performing co-localization calculations, such as "node that can calculate co-localization coefficient (like Pearson’s or Manders’)."

Upon receiving the response from this tool call, we will be able to determine if the suggested node fits our needs and whether it can be connected to the existing segmentation node in the pipeline. If it does, we can proceed to add this node to our pipeline, ensuring that the flow of data and the overall functionality of the pipeline align with the user's requirements.

This step is crucial because it directly addresses the user's need for accurate co-localization calculations after segmentation, which is essential for obtaining meaningful results from the time-series of images.

arguments.prompt: node that can calculate co-localization coefficient (like Pearson’s or Manders’)

response: ij1-colocalization-coloc2

Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

c1838422-4587-470a-9273-0e8a373ed10d.prompt: I have a time-series of images showing the expression of a fluorescent protein in cells, and I want to quantify the co-localization between this protein and a specific organelle over time. The signal is weak and noisy. Can you design a JIPipe pipeline that first applies denoising filters, then accurately segments both the protein and the organelle in each frame, and finally calculates a co-localization coefficient (like Pearson’s or Manders’) for each cell at each time point, outputting a time-series of co-localization values?

arguments.prompt: node that can accurately segment both protein and organelle in each frame

response: ij3d-segmentation-nuclei-3d

This plugin is designed to segment nuclei from cell culture (not from tissues). The method is based on a maximum Z-projection followed by a 2D Segmentation. The segmentation for the 2D projection is based on a global thresholding. The nuclei are then segmented and separated using ImageJ watershed.

This step is crucial because it directly addresses the user's need for accurate co-localization calculations after segmentation, which is essential for obtaining meaningful results from the time-series of images.

arguments.prompt: node that can calculate co-localization coefficient (like Pearson’s or Manders’)

response: ij1-colocalization-coloc2

Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.
